# Transcrição: Simple_Isn't_Always_Better_for_Meta_Ads

- **Fonte:** Simple_Isn't_Always_Better_for_Meta_Ads

## [00:00 - 03:02]

This is the PupCast Shot. I'm John Loomer. Let's thread the needle between too simple and too complicated. Let's go. So if you've been listening to the PupCast for a while, you know all about how I recommend a simplified approach to campaign construction. But I understand the counter arguments. There are exceptions when simple isn't always better. What's important here is that you prioritize a simplified approach while being able to understand the exceptions. So let me explain. In a perfect world, you'll have one campaign and one ad set. Now, it's a perfect world because all of your budget and focus and resources will go into that single ad set. No concerns about watering down your budget by splitting it up between multiple campaigns and ad sets. No auction overlap where you're trying to reach the same people with multiple ad sets and driving up costs. No audience segmentation where you split up your audience to prevent meta from optimizing for the best audience. It's the most efficient approach and you give yourself the best chance of exiting the learning phase. You limit or eliminate all of the various problems related to a lack of volume that come from over-complicating things. But maybe you want to build your email list and sell products and you have the budget to sustain both. Or you have multiple products that are business priorities and they all require dedicated budget. Or you're part of a large company with multiple locations and all of those locations need to get some love. An approach relying on a single campaign and ad set is simply not reasonable or possible for those situations. They are exceptions. Even when you create those multiple campaigns and ad sets, let's prioritize simplicity. Avoid creating unnecessary complexity when possible. Know that each extra campaign or ad set can contribute to all of the problems we've already discussed. Let's move on to another example. Targeting. Now, I strongly advise that you toss aside all of your old school approaches to complex targeting from years past. If you're using detailed targeting and look-alikes, those inputs are almost always used as suggestions now. It's questionable whether they make any impact at all. So there's no reason to obsess over what they are or create separate ad sets based on those inputs. Algorithmic targeting can't be avoided in most cases. So quit fighting it. And there's rarely a reason for remarketing now, especially general remarketing, where you target all of your website visitors or your email list. You're going to prioritize these people when using the broadest targeting inputs.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

And you can prove it with sales campaigns and audience segments. And you rarely need to restrict by age or gender, even though you know that your ideal customer is a woman between the ages of 35 and 54. If this is true and you're optimizing for a purchase, meta will learn from those results and adjust accordingly to show it to people like that. So targeting is something you should keep simplified and avoid unnecessary complexity that often just makes results worse. But there are exceptions. So maybe meta is dedicating too much spend to remarketing when using algorithmic targeting. You want to control that spend. To do so, you'd need to create separate ad sets and define an ad set spending limit on remarketing. Maybe you're getting cheap leads from people over the age of 65. So meta is spending a high percentage of your budget on them. This is something that has happened to me. But you know that very few of these people buy from you. While you could technically fix this by restricting targeting by age, you can also use value rules. Maybe you're running engagement ads to increase the views of your reels and your service helps women who are starting a business. And because those ads are optimized for engagement, you get all kinds of empty engagement from men. So meta starts targeting them more. Now you could restrict your targeting by gender or you could use value rules to better control it. Now about placements. You should keep things simple and use advanced plus placements in most cases. But there are exceptions. If you were to optimize for anything other than conversions, some placements are known to be problematic. Audience network can lead to cheap clicks if you optimize for clicks. Reboarded video can lead to cheap views when you optimize for through play. And ads on Facebook reels can lead to cheap impressions when you optimize for reach. So if you optimize for any of those actions, you absolutely should consider removing those placements. But only then. And you could otherwise use value rules to control it. So here's the bottom of the glass. Yes, you should prioritize a simplified approach. But there are always exceptions when you need to add a layer of complexity. The key here is to thread the needle between too simple and too complicated. Don't create unnecessary complexity that is counterproductive and hurts results. Start as simple as possible and only add complexity if it solves a known problem. One last thing.
